key_insight: The paper argues that ML/AI methods for DFT should be chosen differently depending on whether the target is Kohn-Sham or orbital-free DFT, because the bottlenecks and accuracy needs differ for exchange-correlation, kinetic energy, density, and basis-function learning.
engineering_relevance: For computational materials and nuclear modeling, the right ML strategy can reduce DFT cost while preserving accuracy, but only if it matches the specific functional or representation being approximated.
method: Compare ML targets across DFT: exchange-correlation functional, kinetic energy functional, density, and basis functions.
Favor deep neural networks for Kohn-Sham DFT where they are already strong, but be more cautious for kinetic energy functionals.
Highlight symbolic regression as a promising, conceptually transparent direction, especially for orbital-free and nuclear DFT.
evidence_signal: This is an overview paper synthesizing prior electronic and nuclear DFT work, emphasizing differences in computational bottlenecks and accuracy requirements rather than reporting a single benchmark dataset.
watch_item: Promising ML methods may not transfer directly between Kohn-Sham and orbital-free or from electronic to nuclear DFT, so model choice must be tailored to the target functional and error sensitivity.
paper_title: ML and AI for density functional theory: different priorities for Kohn-Sham and orbital-free DFT, for electronic and nuclear DFT
